source.title: Encoding of conditioned fear in central amygdala inhibitory circuits.

confidence_rationale: Landmark Nature study definitively dissecting CeA internal microcircuit: CeL (lateral) required for fear acquisition, CeM (medial) drives output. Cell-type-specific plasticity in CeL-to-CeM pathway gates fear expression. Supports MECH-074c's fast_prime as a CeM output signal distinct from MECH-046's slower mode-prior write (CeL-mediated cortical channel). Note: same paper already has an entry in targeted_review_sd_035 tagged to SD-035; this entry specifically addresses the MECH-074c fast-priming latency claim.
failure_signatures: CeL-to-CeM pathway plasticity shows phasic/tonic changes but latency of CeM output relative to US onset is not directly quantified in terms comparable to 1-2 sim steps (100-200 ms).
Inhibitory microcircuit model complicates the fast_prime-as-simple-output framing; CeM output is disinhibited, not directly driven.

mapping.source_claim_statement: CeL neuronal activity is required for fear acquisition; CeM drives conditioned fear output responses. Functional circuit analysis shows cell-type-specific phasic/tonic plasticity in the CeL-to-CeM inhibitory pathway gates fear expression and regulates generalization.
mapping.ree_translation: CeM is the anatomical substrate for MECH-074c's fast_prime output: the CeM emits a rapid conditioned response via disinhibition through the CeL-to-CeM inhibitory gate. This is distinct from MECH-046, which operates via a slower cortical mode-prior write pathway.
mapping.mapping_caveat: This paper characterizes the CeL/CeM architecture and plasticity; it does not directly test the temporal signature of fast_prime (1-2 sim steps = ~100-200 ms). The Mendez-Bertolo 2016 paper (already in targeted_review_connectome_mech_046) provides the direct latency evidence.
mapping.source_context: Mouse fear conditioning, in vivo electrophysiology + optogenetics, CeA single-unit recording.

confidence_components.notes: Nature landmark; mouse data generalisable to general mammalian CeA architecture. Latency quantification needed separately.
